text:
This book offers for the first time a truly multidisciplinary exploration of a key period in history - 1917-22 - which was marked by the dissolution of the Austro-Hungarian and the Russian Empires, and which was the time when Ukraine emerged as a self-governing independent state for the first time in its long and varied history. The contributions to the volume, based on extensive research in originally unpublished sources from the military archives in Vienna, Berlin, Munich, Stuttgart and Freiburg, among other places, bring together for the first time in English different perspectives on the wartime and post-war dynamic that enabled Ukrainians to achieve a measure of political independence as the old imperial orders collapsed around them.

This volume is devoted to four principal themes. The first part deals with the military operations of the Central Powers in 1918 and, in particular, with the supplies and organisation of their occupying troops, the administration of occupied territory and the economic exploitation of the country under the terms of the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk. The second part is concerned with the political structure of the period in Ukraine – that is, with the struggle for an independent Ukrainian state on the one hand and with the attempts to bring the whole of the country under Bolshevik control on the other. Finally, in the third and fourth parts, we are concerned with the international political activities of the Entente and of Poland as they tried to exert influence in the emerging political situation in the East in the decisive years of 1917/18. Ultimately, the collection as a whole is intended to contribute – through a dialogue between regional and global history perspectives – to our understanding of the period of limited independence enjoyed by the Ukrainian People's Republic and the workings of the multiple forms of intervention that characterised it and, in general, shaped the contemporary history of Central and Eastern Europe.
